evidence: Action identity ownership is split between `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go` (namespace/action tokens) and `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` (re-exported namespace/action aliases plus ContractID constants).

`goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` keeps backward-compatible alias IDs (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`) alongside canonical IDs, increasing public-surface ambiguity about which identifiers are authoritative.

Governance metadata and compatibility versioning are hardcoded inside per-family spec builders (`protocol_contracts.go`, `startram_contracts.go`) and partially mirrored in tests (`family_specs_test.go`), indicating policy is distributed rather than centrally owned.

Multiple modules define very large callback-bag runtimes (for example `startupInitRuntime`/`StartupRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `workflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, and `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`) where many fields are direct pass-through seams rather than behavior-bearing abstractions.

`goseg/main.go` introduces nested runtime structs (`bootstrapRuntime`, `c2cRuntime`, `serverRuntime`) with repeated nil-guard wrappers (`validate`, `bootstrap`, `startServer`, `runC2CCheck`) that increase indirection cost without reducing domain complexity.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` centralizes many unrelated responsibilities into a single orchestration seam surface, causing broad transitive dependency reach for callers that need only small subsets.

Cross-module image metadata is passed as `map[string]string` (`RuntimeImageOps.GetLatestContainerInfoFn` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`) and consumed via string keys (`repo`, `tag`, `hash`) in both `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` and `goseg/docker/lifecycle/planner.go`.

Several subsystems are anchored to package-level singleton runtimes (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`, `defaultEventRuntime` in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`), creating hidden dependency edges and implicit global state coupling across call paths.

Tests frequently rely on these globals (`goseg/rectify/rectify_test.go`, `goseg/broadcast/state_test.go`, `goseg/broadcast/loop_test.go`) instead of isolated injected runtimes, reinforcing global dependency patterns.

Subsystem orchestration repeats near-identical worker-fanout/fan-in control flow (`RunDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` and `RunVersionSubsystemWithContext` in `goseg/routines/version.go`) instead of a shared seam, so cancellation/error semantics are duplicated and drift-prone.

Boundary translation is scattered: transition publication/runtime policy is re-wired in multiple places (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/config/config_events.go`) rather than centralized in one lifecycle boundary abstraction.

Runtime normalization patterns (`resolve...Runtime`, `default...Runtime`, optional override wiring) are repeated across modules (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`, `goseg/docker/network/network.go`, `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`) with slightly different contracts.
Local implementations are generally readable and defensive with contextual errors (`goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`, `goseg/docker/network/network.go`, `goseg/config/config_events.go`).

There is still recurring local boilerplate (nil-context normalization, runtime defaulting, repeated callback checks) across functions, especially in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` and `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`.
low_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Repeated low-value guard/default scaffolding and duplicated loop orchestration templates reduce local concision.

evidence: All four files are single-line package comments with near-identical template structure ('Package <name> ...'), suggesting standardized boilerplate rather than package-specific intent.
Comments describe broad capability lists but do not document key constraints, ownership boundaries, or non-obvious behavior for these packages.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Package docs are clean but read as generic boilerplate; they should carry sharper, package-specific intent and rationale to avoid AI-style low-signal documentation.

evidence: No deprecated markers, migration TODO/FIXME tags, or old/new API coexistence hints are present in the four reviewed `doc.go` files.
Package comments do not mention transitional compatibility shims or phased migration states.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Absence of migration cues in these doc files is positive, but these files are too thin to provide strong evidence that migration work is fully complete at package level.

evidence: holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows three large flat directories tied to this batch: goseg/config/ (41 files), goseg/shipworkflow/ (49 files), goseg/system/ (36 files), matching prior flat_dirs flags.

goseg/system/disk_shims_test.go is a top-level test shim file in package system that primarily proxies to subpackages groundseg/system/disk and groundseg/system/storage, indicating disk-related test seam logic is not colocated with the disk/storage boundaries it exercises.

In the assigned files, config/bootstrap.go (startup/bootstrap + global mutable state + filesystem/env wiring) and config/config.go (runtime context model + settings snapshots) live in one package root, reinforcing flattened placement rather than bounded subdomains.
`fixtures_test.go` validates fixture existence and only enforces presence of one canonical binding (`C2C connect`), leaving other protocol action bindings as implicit/indirect checks.

`family_specs_test.go` constructs synthetic spec literals (`Name`, `Description`, `Message`) instead of asserting directly against production spec tables, so metadata drift in runtime contract declarations can evade targeted regression checks.

README defines backend quality-gate scope including protocol contracts/conformance, but assigned tests primarily exercise helper validation paths rather than complete contract-governance compatibility invariants.

Runtime dependency injection is implemented as large function-bag structs across modules (for example `RuntimeContainerOps`/`RuntimeStartupOps` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `AccessPointRuntime` in `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go`, and `wifiRuntime` in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`), creating high indirection and repeated default/normalize wiring.

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` concentrates command dispatch, transition planning, config persistence, Docker side effects, Startram alias calls, and broadcast publication in one file, while `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` similarly mixes lifecycle orchestration and event handling concerns.

Config behavior spans parallel models in `goseg/structs/configs.go`, `goseg/config/config.go`, and `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`, requiring manual sync of overlapping fields and section semantics.

Blocking functions are named with a `Start*` prefix in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` (`StartDockerSubsystem`, `StartDockerSubsystemWithRuntime`) while they call `RunDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` and inherit blocking semantics.

Action naming has parallel inverse aliases in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` (`UploadOpenEndpointAction`, `UploadActionOpenEndpoint`) and `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` (`ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`, `UploadOpenEndpointAction`), increasing term ambiguity.
Broadcast loop control flow in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go` uses explicit guards and early returns (`StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime`, `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`) with consistent nil/runtime checks.
Transition application path in `goseg/broadcast/transitions.go` cleanly separates mutation and notify behavior, with deterministic branching for backpressure policy handling.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Several APIs still mix silent no-op returns and error returns for similar invalid states, which makes control-flow intent harder to reason about at call sites.

evidence: `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` maintains typed runtime and state seams and wraps errors with operation-specific context.
`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` uses generics to bind patch fields and parsers, reducing untyped mutation paths.
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Some wire payload boundaries still downcast typed action values to raw strings (`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`), reducing compile-time guarantees at protocol boundaries.

evidence: `StartDockerSubsystem` and `StartDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` are named as non-blocking starts but directly call blocking `RunDockerSubsystemWithRuntime`.
`StartConfigEventLoop` in `goseg/config/config_events.go` is actually non-blocking (spawns goroutine and returns), so similarly named `Start*` APIs expose inconsistent behavioral contracts.

`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` returns `(value, false)` or `nil` on initialization failure in several API functions (`ContractForAction` path via `actionContractForAction`, `UploadActionContracts`, `C2CActionContracts`) without exposing the underlying initialization error.
`ParseAction` in the same file does return wrapped initialization errors, creating mixed failure signaling within one API family.
`goseg/config/config_events.go` logs update errors inside the background loop (`processConfigEvent`) but does not propagate failure to loop caller.
`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go` computes package-level `password` at import time using env/hostname (`resolveAPPassword()`), coupling behavior to process environment before explicit bootstrap.
`goseg/config/config.go` has `init()` that sets runtime context from defaults, and those defaults call environment-sensitive functions (`getBasePath`) via `defaultRuntimeContext()`.

Acronym casing drifts across related config and StarTram code: `EndpointURL` (`goseg/config/config.go`), `UpdateUrl` (`goseg/defaults/defaults.go` via runtime config field assignment), and local `endpointUrl` (`goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go`).
Action naming conventions drift between `Action<Domain><Verb>` and `<Domain><Verb>Action` forms across `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go`.

In `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`, outwardly similar query APIs expose mixed contracts: some return typed errors (`ParseAction`), while others silently collapse initialization failures to empty results (`UploadActionContracts`, `actionContractsForNamespace`, `ContractForAction` via bool-only contract).
`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` exposes `Start*` and `Run*` names where both `StartDockerSubsystem*` variants are blocking, creating unstable mental model for callers.
`goseg/leak/broadcast.go` applies auth-level scoping for unauthorized channels by sending per-patp filtered payloads and full payloads only when `status.Auth` is true.
No direct contradictory authorization mechanism was observed in the reviewed cross-cutting files; transport-level filtering pattern appears consistent in the inspected leak/broadcast path.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Authorization decisions rely on shared runtime status maps and are not uniformly represented as explicit policy abstractions across all reviewed entry points.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity has no single authority, with duplicated token/ID ownership across actionids and contracts layers.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines canonical namespace/action strings, while `protocol_contracts.go` redefines typed aliases from them and derives separate ContractID constants.
`protocol_contracts.go` carries compatibility aliases for contract IDs, so multiple symbols can represent the same contract, which blurs the stable public surface.
Conformance checks in `fixtures_test.go` validate bindings through `actions` symbols, not a single registry exported by contracts, reinforcing split ownership boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Create one canonical protocol-contract registry module that owns namespace/action tokens, contract IDs, and metadata; make `actionids` or contracts a thin compatibility adapter (deprecated path) and migrate callers/tests to the single registry API.

findings.summary: Contract governance policy is embedded per file/family, producing scattered ownership and versioning rules.
evidence: Both protocol and startram spec builders hardcode `OwnerModule`, `Stability`, and `SinceVersion` literals in each entry.
`family_specs_test.go` reconstructs governance expectations inside ad-hoc specs, coupling tests to repeated policy fields rather than a shared policy source.
`actions_test.go` verifies action/contract alignment locally, adding another point where contract policy consistency is validated indirectly instead of via one governing catalog contract.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared governance policy layer (e.g., central metadata provider + validation rules) consumed by all family spec builders and alignment tests, so ownership/version policy changes are made once and enforced uniformly.

findings.summary: Oversized callback-bag runtimes add indirection but weak domain leverage.
evidence: Runtime structs aggregate many callbacks spanning unrelated concerns (startup init, networking, storage, transition publishing, workflow dispatch) and are frequently passed wholesale.
Several wrapper methods only validate/delegate callbacks with nil checks, increasing call depth without adding policy or translation.
Large seam surfaces make partial reuse difficult and force modules to depend on broad runtime shapes.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into capability-focused interfaces (for example: config mutation, transition publication, container lifecycle, storage I/O), inject only required capabilities per workflow, and move repeated nil-guard delegation into shared seam validators/builders.

findings.summary: Image selection uses untyped map contracts across modules.
evidence: `GetLatestContainerInfoFn` returns `map[string]string`, and consumers read hard-coded keys (`repo`, `tag`, `hash`) to build image refs.
No compile-time contract guarantees those keys exist or are valid; missing/renamed keys silently produce malformed image references.
The same implicit key protocol is duplicated in multiple modules.
findings.suggestion: Replace `map[string]string` with a typed struct (for example `ImageDescriptor{Repo, Tag, Digest}`), return that type from runtime image seams, and centralize image reference formatting in one helper used by orchestration and lifecycle.

findings.summary: Package-level default runtimes create hidden global dependency edges.
evidence: Core behaviors rely on package globals (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime`, `defaultEventRuntime`) instead of explicit runtime ownership.
Callers often fall back to defaults when runtime is nil, so dependency selection is implicit and context-sensitive.
Tests frequently mutate/read global default state, which couples test behavior to process-level singleton lifecycle.
findings.suggestion: Define explicit runtime owners at subsystem boundaries, require runtime injection in new entrypoints, and keep globals only as thin compatibility adapters that delegate to injected runtime instances.

findings.summary: Worker lifecycle orchestration is duplicated across subsystems.
evidence: Subsystems repeatedly implement the same pattern: spawn worker goroutines, collect errors on a channel, cancel context, waitgroup join, return first failure.
This choreography is implemented independently with local variants rather than via one reusable lifecycle runner.
Error/cancellation behavior becomes policy-by-copy, which increases seam friction when evolving subsystem startup/teardown semantics.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared subsystem runner utility (context fanout, first-error cancel, graceful shutdown join) and migrate version/docker/startup service loops to it so lifecycle semantics are standardized in one place.

findings.summary: Package doc comments follow a repetitive template and under-specify package intent, creating low-signal AI-like documentation debt.
evidence: All comments use the same pattern ('Package X ...') with capability lists but little unique guidance.
None of the docs describe boundary decisions (what is intentionally out-of-scope), which limits reader value despite being syntactically correct.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite package comments to include one concrete boundary statement and one non-obvious design intent per package (for example: key ownership, exclusion scope, or lifecycle guarantees), while keeping comments concise.

findings.summary: Core directories are oversized and flat, obscuring domain boundaries.
evidence: Packet structure profile reports goseg/config/ (41 files), goseg/shipworkflow/ (49), goseg/system/ (36), each flagged as flat_dirs in holistic context.
Assigned files show unrelated concerns coexisting at top level (bootstrap/runtime context/models/shims) instead of foldered domain slices.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create target folders `goseg/config/runtime`, `goseg/config/bootstrap`, `goseg/system/storagetest`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle`; (2) move lowest-risk files first (`shipworkflow/lifecycle*.go` into lifecycle, then `system/disk_*_test.go` into storagetest or disk/storage package tests), then split config bootstrap/runtime files; (3) update package names/imports with `goimports -w goseg/...`; (4) validate with `go test ./goseg/config ./goseg/shipworkflow ./goseg/system` and finally `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Disk compatibility shims are placed in root system tests instead of disk/storage test boundaries.
evidence: disk_shims_test.go defines many pass-through functions (parseFstabLine, ListHardDisks, CreateGroundSegFilesystem, etc.) delegating to `system/disk` and `system/storage`.
disk_health_test.go uses those shims from package system, coupling root-level tests to subpackage internals through a compatibility layer.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) move disk-oriented tests to subpackage targets `goseg/system/disk` and `goseg/system/storage`; (2) replace root shims with direct subpackage test helpers (`*_test.go` local helpers) and delete transitional shims last; (3) update imports via `goimports -w goseg/system/...`; (4) validate incrementally with `go test ./goseg/system/disk ./goseg/system/storage` then `go test ./goseg/system/...`.

findings.summary: Config package combines bootstrap orchestration and runtime context modeling in one ownership surface.
evidence: bootstrap.go handles init sequencing, env/argv reads, persistence writes, and global readiness flags.
config.go defines runtime context core model, context mutation API, and multiple settings snapshot types in the same package scope.
Tests in bootstrap_test.go are tied to process-level concerns (env/argv), confirming mixed architectural roles under one package root.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) split package ownership into `config/runtimectx` (RuntimeContext + setters/getters), `config/bootstrap` (Initialize/bootstrap flow), and `config/state` (global config snapshots); (2) move runtime context types/functions first, then bootstrap orchestration, then state accessors; (3) update call sites/imports with `goimports -w goseg/...` and `go test ./goseg/config/...`; (4) run `go test ./...` to confirm no boot-order regressions.

findings.summary: Contract governance policy is split between docs, metadata helpers, and hard-coded spec literals.
evidence: README declares governance/quality-gate policy locations, but runtime compatibility fields remain embedded in code literals.
`family_specs_test.go` references `contractMetadataFor(...).IntroducedIn` while production specs use fixed date strings (`2026.03.02`, `2026.01.20`), creating multiple policy-authoring paths.
Architecture contracts become harder to audit because compatibility semantics are duplicated across runtime spec constructors and separate governance surfaces.
findings.suggestion: Unify governance metadata authoring by making contract spec builders consume a single canonical metadata registry (e.g., through `contractMetadataFor` for all `SinceVersion`/owner/stability fields), then keep README governance docs as pointers to that canonical runtime source and verification commands.

findings.summary: Conformance tests assert one canonical action path and miss full binding completeness checks.
evidence: `TestActionContractFixturesCoverCanonicalBindings` only hard-requires `c2c:connect` presence and generic validation loops; it does not explicitly enforce all known action IDs from protocol declarations.
Action universe in `actionids.go` and contract specs includes upload reset/open-endpoint and c2c connect, but fixture tests do not assert complete one-to-one coverage against that declared universe.
A newly added action could be omitted from fixtures and still pass if existing generic checks remain satisfied.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit completeness test that derives expected action bindings from the authoritative contract/action list and compares set equality with fixture bindings (missing and unexpected both fail); keep unknown-action rejection as a separate negative test.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity is spread across actions/actionids/contracts, obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `actionids` defines raw namespace/action tokens; `contracts` re-wraps them as local typed aliases; conformance tests consume a different package (`actions`) for canonical values.

Directory profile shows `goseg/protocol/contracts/` already at 11 mixed files, while protocol identity constants are still split out into an extra sibling package, increasing cross-package hopping for one concept.
This organization increases coupling for simple compatibility changes (token rename/addition touches multiple packages and tests).
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1: choose a single owner for protocol action identity (target `goseg/protocol/actions/`) and define canonical typed namespace/verb constants there. Stage 2: move or replace `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go` with compatibility aliases in `actions` (or deprecate with temporary forwarding file). Stage 3: update `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and conformance tests to import only the chosen owner package; remove duplicate local alias constants where possible. Stage 4: validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/actions ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/conformance` then `go test ./...` to ensure import and contract consistency after moves.

findings.summary: Dependency seams are represented by oversized callback bags across subsystems, increasing indirection and reducing coherent module boundaries.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many callback structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, etc.) and separate default builders, spreading one runtime concept across many loosely related bags.
`accesspoint.go` combines mutable config values with operational callbacks in `AccessPointRuntime`, then mirrors this in `normalizeAccessPointRuntime` with a long nil-default sequence.
`wifi_runtime_methods.go` repeats the same pattern via `wifiRuntime`, `NewWiFiRuntime`, and `NewWiFiRuntimeWith` fallback behavior.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to smaller capability interfaces per concern (config source, process control, network scanning, container lifecycle) and compose explicit runtime dependencies at boundary constructors instead of package-wide callback bags.

findings.summary: Core orchestration files bundle transport/event plumbing with domain transitions and side effects, blurring responsibility boundaries.
evidence: `urbit_operations_runtime.go` includes command token mapping, transition step construction, config mutation flows, Docker operations, and post-transition broadcast behavior in one unit.
`subsystem_events.go` handles health loop lifecycle, Docker event stream consumption, bus backpressure behavior, and transition failure window accounting together.
`actions.go` keeps protocol contract initialization and metadata assembly separate, but transition command mapping remains duplicated in workflow runtime instead of a single protocol-driven registry.
findings.suggestion: Split orchestration into layers: protocol command registry, pure transition planners, and infrastructure executors. Move transition command mapping to a shared contract/registry so workflow runtime consumes typed plans rather than command strings.

findings.summary: Configuration schema, runtime snapshots, and patch/update schema are modeled in parallel, creating systemic drift risk.
evidence: `structs/configs.go` defines persisted section structures and section update methods for connectivity/startram/runtime/auth/penpai.
`config.go` defines separate snapshot-oriented settings structs (`StartramSettings`, `Check502Settings`, `ImportSettings`, etc.) plus runtime context wrappers.
`config_update_schema.go` introduces another patch model (`ConnectivityPatch`, `RuntimePatch`, etc.) and parser/apply/merge registry that must stay aligned with both struct layers.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical section schema descriptor (or codegen-backed model) used to derive persisted structs, update patches, and snapshot views, reducing manual field-by-field synchronization.

findings.summary: Tests repeatedly build ad hoc seam/reset scaffolding per package, reflecting inconsistent runtime testing architecture.
evidence: `accesspoint_test.go` has multiple package-specific reset helpers (`resetParameterGlobalsForTest`, `resetRouterGlobalsForTest`) and command-capture setup logic.
`importer_test.go` defines separate runtime accessor helpers (`initRuntime`, `uploadRuntime`, `provisionRuntime`) and custom session/runtime reset utilities.
`startup_orchestrator_test.go` builds a large callback-setter map and runtime-option DSL to compensate for broad callback seams.
`wifi_test.go` repeats seam reset/runtime construction patterns (`resetWifiSeamsForTest`, `testWifiRuntime`).
findings.suggestion: Standardize seam-test patterns via shared internal test runtime builders for callback wiring/reset lifecycle, so packages reuse one approach and avoid package-local harness DSLs.

findings.summary: Docker subsystem `Start*` APIs violate their implied non-blocking contract by delegating to blocking `Run*` execution.
evidence: `StartDockerSubsystem` and `StartDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` directly call `RunDockerSubsystemWithRuntime`.
The same file documents `RunDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` as blocking until cancellation/failure.
Other `Start*` APIs in reviewed files (for example `StartConfigEventLoop`, `StartBroadcastLoop`) are non-blocking, so caller expectations are inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Either rename blocking methods to `Run*` only and remove `Start*` aliases, or make `StartDockerSubsystem*` actually asynchronous by spawning goroutines and returning immediately with lifecycle handles.

findings.summary: Action contract APIs silently suppress initialization failures in several read paths, while siblings return explicit errors.
evidence: `ParseAction` returns initialization errors from `ensureActionContractsInitialized`.
`actionContractForAction`, `actionContractsForNamespace`, `UploadActionContracts`, and `C2CActionContracts` return false/nil when initialization fails, losing root cause context.
The contract subsystem performs strict validation and wraps rich errors, but those errors are not consistently surfaced through the actions API.
findings.suggestion: Standardize all public actions read APIs to return `(value, error)` (or typed result+error) and preserve initialization/validation errors consistently; keep bool-only results for pure membership checks only.

findings.summary: Environment- and host-dependent values are computed at package initialization time, coupling behavior to import order and process startup context.
evidence: `accesspoint` package-level `password` is initialized from `resolveAPPassword()` which reads env and hostname.
`config.init()` eagerly sets runtime context; default context uses environment-sensitive base path resolution through `getBasePath`.
These values are established before explicit bootstrap orchestration, making tests and embedding scenarios sensitive to import-time environment.
findings.suggestion: Move environment/hostname resolution into explicit bootstrap/runtime construction paths and keep package-level globals as inert defaults; inject resolved values through runtime structs.

findings.summary: Parallel inverse aliases for the same action concepts create ambiguous naming vocabulary across protocol layers.
evidence: Both `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint` and `UploadOpenEndpointAction` exist in actions package.
Contracts package additionally exports `UploadOpenEndpointAction` and backward alias `UploadActionOpenEndpoint`.
The same semantic concept is represented in both `<Domain><Verb>Action` and `Action<Domain><Verb>` forms.
findings.suggestion: Pick one canonical naming order for action symbols across `actionids`, `actions`, and `contracts` packages and deprecate transitional aliases behind a documented migration window.

findings.summary: The actions API exposes mixed query semantics (errorful vs silent) for similar contract lookup operations.
evidence: `ParseAction` communicates initialization failure explicitly via error.
`SupportedActions`, `ActionMetas`, `ContractForAction`, and contract-slice helpers return empty/false results on hidden initialization failure paths.
Tests assert behavior for contract relationships but do not enforce a single error contract shape across all public query APIs.
findings.suggestion: Define one API contract rule for public action queries (for example, all initialization-dependent lookups return errors) and align tests to enforce that contract uniformly.

findings.summary: Acronym and term style conventions drift across neighboring modules, creating style islands in core runtime/config code.
evidence: Config-facing fields and locals vary between `EndpointURL`, `UpdateUrl`, and `endpointUrl` forms.
The inconsistency appears inside tightly related connectivity/startram config paths rather than isolated legacy adapters.
findings.suggestion: Adopt and enforce a single acronym casing convention (for example `URL`) across exported fields, locals, and config patch keys in config/defaults/rectify modules.
